text:
HEAD AND NECK ONCOLOGY -- SURVEILLANCE FOLLOW-UP NOTE
3-Month Post-Treatment Assessment

ATTENDING: Head and Neck Medical Oncology
VISIT TYPE: Return Visit -- Post-Treatment Surveillance

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT:
Ms. Margaret Okonkwo is a 52-year-old Nigerian-American female with a history of HPV-positive (p16+) oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma of the left base of tongue (T2N1M0, AJCC 8th edition Stage I) who presents for her 3-month post-chemoradiation surveillance visit.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
Ms. Okonkwo completed definitive concurrent chemoradiotherapy approximately 3 months ago. As a brief reminder of her treatment course: she received 70 Gy in 35 fractions via VMAT-IMRT with a simultaneous integrated boost to the high-risk planning target volume (primary base of tongue mass and involved left level IIA node), with concurrent cisplatin 100 mg/m2 on days 1 and 22, and a dose-reduced third cycle at 75 mg/m2 on day 43 (reduced due to grade 2 nephrotoxicity and grade 3 mucositis at the time of cycle 3). She was admitted during week 4 of treatment for grade 3 mucositis, requiring IV hydration, IV PCA morphine for pain control, and escalation to full enteral nutrition via her PEG tube. She was discharged after a 5-day inpatient stay with improvement. She completed all 35 radiation fractions without interruption.

At approximately 10 weeks post-treatment, she underwent response assessment PET-CT, which demonstrated a complete metabolic response at both the primary left base of tongue site and the previously involved left level IIA lymph node. This was confirmed by clinical flexible laryngoscopy showing healing mucosa at the base of tongue with no gross residual tumor, and no palpable cervical lymphadenopathy on examination at that visit. She was seen in multidisciplinary follow-up at that time and counseled on her excellent response.

Today, at the 3-month mark, she presents reporting substantial functional improvement. Most notably, she has successfully transitioned to full oral intake. Her PEG tube was removed approximately 2 weeks ago by the interventional gastroenterology service without complication, following a stepwise reduction in tube feeding volumes as her oral intake improved. She is currently eating a soft mechanical diet and tolerating thickened liquids, with ongoing management by her speech-language pathologist. She describes her swallowing as "slow but manageable" and reports that she takes her time with meals. She is working with her speech therapist on exercises to improve base-of-tongue retraction and pharyngeal clearance.

She continues to note grade 1 xerostomia, which she describes as a persistent dry mouth sensation that is worse in the morning and during speaking for prolonged periods -- relevant given her occupation as a high school English teacher. She is using Biotene oral rinse and is drinking water frequently throughout the day. She has not been using salivary substitutes consistently but was encouraged to do so at prior visits.

She denies any new throat pain, odynophagia, hemoptysis, globus sensation, new neck masses, or palpable lumps. She denies otalgia bilaterally. No hoarseness or voice changes. No epistaxis. She does not report any new neurologic symptoms, such as facial numbness or shoulder weakness. She has resumed part-time teaching duties and finds that her voice fatigues after extended periods of classroom instruction but is otherwise functional.

She denies fever, chills, night sweats, or unintentional weight loss. Weight is slowly recovering. She was 68 kg at baseline, dropped to 63 kg at the end of treatment, and is now at 65 kg, reflecting approximately 2 kg of recovery from nadir.

Hypothyroidism is stable; she is on levothyroxine at her pre-treatment dose. No symptoms of hypothyroidism reported (no cold intolerance, fatigue beyond the expected post-treatment state, or constipation).

Gastroesophageal reflux is managed with her usual regimen and not significantly symptomatic.

No new medications have been started.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS:
- Constitutional: Fatigue present but improving; ECOG PS 1. No fever, no night sweats. Appetite recovering.
- HEENT: Dry mouth (grade 1 xerostomia), mild dysphagia to solids as described, no throat pain at rest, no new neck masses, no otalgia, no epistaxis, no visual changes.
- Endocrine: Hypothyroidism stable on levothyroxine.
- Cardiovascular: No chest pain, no palpitations, no lower extremity edema.
- Pulmonary: No dyspnea, no cough.
- GI: GERD stable. Normal bowel habits. PEG tube site healed without signs of infection or leakage.
- Renal: No dysuria. Creatinine had risen to 1.4 mg/dL at end of treatment (grade 2 nephrotoxicity) and was improving at the 10-week visit.
- Musculoskeletal: No new joint pain or myalgias. Mild left neck stiffness, which she attributes to radiation changes, but improving.
- Neurologic: No focal deficits. No shoulder weakness (monitoring for CN XI injury given left neck irradiation). No facial numbness.
- Skin: Left neck radiation dermatitis resolving; she reports the skin is peeling but improving.
- Psychiatric: She is adjusting well emotionally to her diagnosis and treatment. She is grateful for the complete response and is engaging with her support network.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:

General: Well-appearing, well-nourished female in no acute distress. Alert and oriented, conversational, affect appropriate. Mildly reduced energy compared to pre-illness baseline but overall improved from treatment completion.

Vital Signs: Within normal limits. Weight: 65 kg (BMI 23.1). No fever.

Head/Face: No facial asymmetry. Temporomandibular joint non-tender bilaterally. Mouth opening full, no trismus.

Oral Cavity/Oropharynx: Oral mucosa moist, no active pseudomembranous ulceration. Residual mucosal erythema consistent with resolving radiation mucositis. Dentition with ongoing fluoride treatment appliances in place. Salivary pooling reduced but some xerostomia evident on exam. Tongue mobile, no visible lesion. Hard and soft palate intact.

Neck: No palpable cervical lymphadenopathy in levels I through V bilaterally. Left neck reveals soft tissue induration consistent with post-radiation fibrosis and resolving skin changes -- peeling, hyperpigmented radiation field extending from angle of mandible down to the supraclavicular fossa on the left. No palpable masses. No tenderness on palpation of the left neck. Trachea midline. Thyroid gland non-palpable, non-tender.

Flexible Nasopharyngolaryngoscopy (performed in clinic): Nasal passages patent bilaterally. Nasopharynx: no mass, no adenoid hypertrophy. Oropharynx: base of tongue shows healing mucosa with mucosal thickening consistent with post-radiation changes; no discrete ulceration, no exophytic mass, no suspicious lesion identified. Valleculae clear. Epiglottis normal. Bilateral arytenoids mobile. True vocal cords mobile bilaterally with full abduction and adduction; no lesions. No supraglottic or glottic mass. Piriform sinuses clear bilaterally. Mild pooling of secretions in the hypopharynx consistent with impaired clearance in the setting of post-radiation dysmotility, but no gross aspiration during the examination.

Shoulder: Range of motion full bilaterally. Shoulder shrug strength 5/5 bilaterally (CN XI intact).

Skin: Resolving radiation dermatitis of the left neck. No new skin lesions.

Neurological: CN II-XII grossly intact. No focal motor or sensory deficits.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS:

Complete Blood Count: Within normal limits. WBC 5.8 K/uL, Hgb 12.9 g/dL (mild normocytic anemia, stable and improving from treatment-related nadir), Plt 218 K/uL.

Comprehensive Metabolic Panel: Sodium 139, Potassium 4.0, CO2 26, BUN 14, Creatinine 1.0 mg/dL (returned to near-baseline from nadir of 1.4 mg/dL at end of treatment; GFR approximately 60 mL/min -- reassuring trajectory). ALT 22, AST 19, Alk Phos 78, Total Bilirubin 0.5. Albumin 3.8 g/dL (recovering from treatment-related hypoalbuminemia).

Magnesium: 1.9 mg/dL (normal; had required IV repletion during chemoradiation for magnesium wasting).

Thyroid Stimulating Hormone (TSH): 1.8 mIU/L (within normal range; radiation-induced hypothyroidism remains a known long-term risk -- will continue to monitor given left-sided neck irradiation including thyroid field). Patient remains on levothyroxine 75 mcg daily at pre-treatment dose.

---

IMAGING:

The response assessment PET-CT performed approximately 10 weeks post-chemoradiation (reviewed again today) demonstrated complete metabolic response at the primary left base of tongue site and at the previously involved left level IIA lymph node, with no residual FDG-avid disease. No distant metastatic disease was identified. These findings are consistent with a robust complete response to definitive chemoradiation.

No new imaging was obtained today. Per standard surveillance protocol for HPV-positive oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma with confirmed complete metabolic response, routine CT of the neck with contrast will be obtained at the 6-month post-treatment mark (approximately 3 months from today).

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN:

1. HPV-POSITIVE OROPHARYNGEAL SQUAMOUS CELL CARCINOMA, LEFT BASE OF TONGUE, T2N1M0 (AJCC 8th edition Stage I), status post definitive concurrent IMRT + cisplatin -- complete metabolic response confirmed at 10-week post-treatment PET-CT:
   - Patient is currently without clinical or radiographic evidence of disease. No palpable lymphadenopathy and laryngoscopy does not demonstrate residual or recurrent tumor.
   - Clinical surveillance will continue. Next scheduled CT neck and chest with contrast at 6 months post-chemoradiation (approximately 3 months from today). Given complete metabolic response and favorable HPV-positive biology, PET-CT is not routinely indicated beyond the initial response assessment scan in the absence of clinical suspicion for recurrence.
   - If the patient develops new neck mass, worsening throat pain, unexplained weight loss, otalgia, or other concerning symptoms, she has been instructed to call the office immediately and not wait for her next scheduled appointment.
   - She will continue to be seen in our head and neck oncology clinic every 3 months for the first 2 years following treatment per standard surveillance intervals.

2. DYSPHAGIA, POST-RADIATION:
   - Patient has made excellent progress, transitioning from full PEG tube dependence at end of treatment to complete oral intake and successful PEG tube removal. This is a meaningful functional milestone.
   - Current diet: soft mechanical diet, thickened liquids. She is working with her speech-language pathologist (SLP) twice weekly on base-of-tongue strengthening and pharyngeal clearance exercises (Shaker exercises, Mendelsohn maneuver, effortful swallow protocol).
   - Will discuss with SLP at next joint visit whether a modified barium swallow study (MBS) is warranted to formally characterize swallowing physiology and guide dietary advancement. Goal is to liberalize diet texture as function continues to improve.
   - Encouraged to maintain compliance with swallowing therapy -- disuse of the swallowing musculature carries the risk of further fibrosis and dysfunction.

3. XEROSTOMIA, GRADE 1:
   - Mild but persistent dry mouth, expected late effect from bilateral parotid irradiation (mean parotid doses within QUANTEC constraints but not zero). Of note, right parotid mean dose 22 Gy and left parotid 24 Gy during treatment.
   - Counseled on the importance of consistent use of salivary substitutes and oral moisturizing agents. Recommended Biotene Oral Balance gel for nighttime use in addition to daytime spray. Xylitol-containing products (gum, mints) can provide modest salivary stimulation; recommended as an adjunct.
   - Encouraged excellent dental hygiene and continued use of daily fluoride trays to prevent radiation caries (high-risk late complication given field of radiation). Dental oncology follow-up confirmed.
   - Discussed that xerostomia may improve somewhat over the coming 12-18 months as residual salivary gland function recovers, though some degree of chronic xerostomia is expected.

4. RADIATION DERMATITIS / POST-RADIATION SKIN CHANGES, LEFT NECK -- RESOLVING:
   - Current appearance is consistent with resolution of acute grade 2 radiation dermatitis with residual hyperpigmentation and peeling skin. No signs of moist desquamation or secondary infection.
   - Recommended gentle skin care: unscented moisturizing lotion (e.g., Lubriderm or Aquaphor) twice daily to the left neck radiation field. Avoid sun exposure to the irradiated skin field without broad-spectrum SPF 50+ sunscreen.
   - Will monitor for late radiation fibrosis of the soft tissue and subcutaneous tissues of the neck over subsequent surveillance visits.

5. NEPHROTOXICITY, POST-CISPLATIN -- RESOLVING:
   - Creatinine has returned to 1.0 mg/dL (baseline 0.8 mg/dL), representing near-complete renal recovery following cisplatin-related nephrotoxicity. GFR approximately 60 mL/min.
   - Will continue to trend renal function at each surveillance visit. Long-term monitoring for progressive chronic kidney disease given cumulative cisplatin exposure (2 full doses + 1 reduced dose).
   - Encouraged excellent hydration.

6. HYPOTHYROIDISM:
   - TSH 1.8 on current levothyroxine 75 mcg daily -- euthyroid. No dose adjustment needed at this time.
   - Radiation-induced hypothyroidism is a well-recognized late effect in patients who receive neck irradiation that includes the thyroid field. Risk is approximately 25-50% at 5 years. Will continue annual TSH monitoring; if TSH rises above 4.5 and/or patient becomes symptomatic, will adjust levothyroxine accordingly.

7. GENERAL HEALTH AND SURVIVORSHIP:
   - Weight recovering (65 kg, up from treatment nadir of 63 kg; pre-treatment baseline was 68 kg). Encouraged continued nutritional rehabilitation and protein-rich diet.
   - She has resumed part-time teaching. Will likely be able to return to full-time in the near future given her current trajectory.
   - Discussed the importance of maintaining physical activity to combat fatigue and promote overall recovery.
   - She expressed interest in a head and neck cancer survivorship support group and in the HPV-positive oropharyngeal cancer community. Provided referrals to the survivorship clinic and the institution's cancer support services.
   - Counseled on smoking and alcohol avoidance (she is a never-smoker and does not drink alcohol -- favorable prognostic features already in place).
   - Discussed the role of HPV vaccination -- while she is above the routine vaccination age, her school-aged children may benefit from vaccination (family counseling provided).

RETURN VISIT: 3 months (6-month post-treatment mark). CT neck and chest with contrast to be ordered prior to that visit. She was given the nursing triage phone line and instructed to call if any new or concerning symptoms arise in the interim.

Time spent with patient: 45 minutes, of which greater than 50% was spent in counseling and coordination of care.

Electronically signed: Attending Physician, Head and Neck Medical Oncology
